project.name: lifegroups
project.description: A web app for tracking Life Groups: leader weekly check-ins, attendance, group health, guest pipeline, follow-ups, a ministry-wide calendar, and admin operations. Note: this project has over 100 source files; consider scoping analysis to a subdirectory for faster results.

tour.title: Project Overview & Roadmap
tour.description: Start with the README to learn what LifeGroups is: an operations dashboard that tracks life-group leader weekly check-ins, attendance, group health, the guest pipeline, follow-ups, a ministry calendar, and admin/shepherd-care work. The README lays out the public-vs-protected routing split, the role model, the personas, and the Supabase backing store, giving you the map this tour follows. The current product direction is 'Julian's admin operating system' centered on shepherd care and launch planning.

tour.title: App Entry & Auth Boundary
tour.description: Every request first hits the Next.js root layout, which loads fonts and wraps all routes in the HTML shell, and then the root middleware, which delegates to the Supabase session refresher on each request. This middleware is the cross-cutting auth boundary: it keeps the Supabase cookie session fresh before any page or server action runs, and its matcher skips static assets. This is where the 'follow a real request' story begins.
tour.languageLesson: Next.js App Router runs middleware.ts at the edge before route handlers. The root layout.tsx is a Server Component that renders once around every route segment, so it is the natural place for global fonts, metadata, and the html/body shell.

tour.title: Supabase Client & Configuration
tour.description: Continuing the request, server code talks to the database through a server-side Supabase client factory bound to Next.js cookies, configured by an env-reading module that validates Supabase settings and exposes an isSupabaseConfigured guard. This pairing is the seam between the app and Postgres: the config module fails loudly when env vars are missing, and the server factory hands every server component and action an authenticated, cookie-scoped client.

tour.title: Sessions, Roles & Rate Limiting
tour.description: With a Supabase client in hand, the auth layer enforces who may proceed. The session module exposes getCurrentSession plus role-guard helpers (requireAdmin, requireSuperAdmin, requireLeader) used across every protected route and action, while the roles module defines the user_role enum, role predicates, default landing paths, and role-scoped navigation. Rate limiting protects the unauthenticated forgot-password flow with an Upstash sliding window that fails open when Redis is absent. Together these form the Auth & Security layer guarding the rest of the app.
tour.languageLesson: These role guards are async functions that throw or redirect when the caller lacks a role. Calling requireAdmin() at the top of a Server Component is the App Router idiom for a route gate: the guard runs server-side before any data is fetched or rendered.

tour.title: Types & Design Tokens (Foundations)
tour.description: Before the data layer, note the foundational contracts every layer imports. types/database.ts is a hand-written mirror of the Postgres schema (rows, inserts, enums), and types/enums.ts holds shared union types like pipeline stages and statuses. lib/pastoral.ts is the most depended-upon file in the codebase (fan-in 87): the pastoral design-system tokens (fonts, palette, textures) shared across the entire UI kit. These are imported-only foundations that pin down the vocabulary used everywhere else.
tour.languageLesson: TypeScript union types from string-literal arrays (e.g. pipeline stages) give you both a compile-time type and a runtime value list, so the same source drives validation and exhaustive switch handling across UI and data layers.

tour.title: Data Access: Read Models
tour.description: The read-model layer is the project's primary data-access surface: roughly fifty typed Supabase query functions covering groups, attendance, guests, follow-ups, metrics, audit events, shepherd care, and launch planning. Every protected page reads through here rather than querying Supabase directly, so reads stay typed against the contracts from Step 5 and consistently flow through Row-Level Security. This is the read half of the Domain & Data-Access layer.

tour.title: Domain Logic: Dashboards & Metrics
tour.description: On top of raw reads, the domain modules compute meaning. The dashboard data layer assembles admin and leader payloads (health, capacity, attendance, attention items) from the read models, backed by shared dashboard types and centralized label maps that translate enum codes to display text. Supporting domain libraries compute effective capacity and health status (metrics), validate admin write payloads (validation), and generate recurring meeting occurrences (calendar). This is where read-model data becomes the product's operational signals.

tour.title: Server Actions: Results & Instrumentation
tour.description: Writes flow back through a small, consistent action toolkit. The action-result helpers (actionOk/actionFail) and RPC error mapping give every admin server action a uniform success/failure envelope, validation type-guards normalize and reject bad payloads before they reach the database RPCs, and the instrumentation helper wraps actions in a correlation-scoped logging context with elapsed timing. This trio standardizes how mutations report success, get validated, and get observed.

tour.title: Protected & Admin Route Gates
tour.description: Now the UI surfaces. The protected route-group layout enforces an authenticated session and redirects unauthenticated or password-reset-required users, while the admin layout further gates on requireAdmin (Step 4) and wraps admin pages in the app-shell chrome. These nested layouts are how the README's public-vs-protected and admin-vs-leader split is actually realized: the gate is the layout, so no admin page renders without passing the role check.
tour.languageLesson: App Router nested layouts compose top-down: the (protected) group layout runs its auth check, then the admin layout runs requireAdmin, before any child page. Route groups in parentheses organize segments without adding URL path segments.

tour.title: Admin Surface: Dashboard & Operations
tour.description: The admin dashboard server page gates on admin role and fetches dashboard data for the selected week, then hands off to the DashboardClient, which composes the week selector, summary tiles, attention queue, capacity, health, follow-ups, and setup-gap panels. This is the operational home screen built from the domain payloads of Step 7, demonstrating the server-fetch then client-compose pattern used across the admin surface.

tour.title: Admin Surface: Shepherd Care & Launch Planning
tour.description: The current product direction lives in two admin routes. Shepherd Care resolves search params, loads the care directory, coverage, and interaction data, and renders the care dashboard, while Launch Planning loads assumptions, group inputs, and scenarios and computes a launch plan. Both follow the same pattern as Step 10 and represent the 'Julian's admin operating system' focus the README highlights.

tour.title: Leader Surface: Dashboard, Check-in & Calendar
tour.description: The other half of the protected app is for group leaders. The leader landing page authorizes the leader, loads dashboard metrics and follow-ups, and renders group cards; the weekly check-in page assembles members, prior attendance, health, and metric defaults and computes check-in due status; the leader calendar authorizes the leader for a group and renders month occurrences. These routes are role-gated to leader/co-leader and are where the weekly check-in workflow the README centers on actually happens.

tour.title: Database Schema & Row-Level Security
tour.description: Everything above rests on Postgres. The Phase 2 baseline migration creates the 13 core operational tables (profiles, groups, members, attendance, guests, follow-ups, audit) plus their enums, the Phase 4 migration defines the auth helper functions (auth_role, auth_is_admin, auth_is_leader_of) and enables the RLS policies that enforce access at the database level, and later phase migrations add the write RPCs the server actions call: leader check-in submission and the shepherd-care foundation. This closes the loop from the route gates in Step 9 down to the rows they protect.
tour.languageLesson: Supabase RLS enforces authorization in the database itself: policies use SQL helper functions (auth_role(), auth_is_admin()) so a leader can only read their own group's rows even if the app code were bypassed. Writes go through SECURITY DEFINER RPCs, keeping mutation logic and audit logging server-side. Migrations are timestamp-ordered and applied incrementally.

tour.title: CI/CD & Contributor Guidance
tour.description: Finally, the capstone: the GitHub Actions CI pipeline defines how code is tested on each change, and AGENTS.md sets the review priorities for the repo, emphasizing P0/P1 correctness, privacy, auth, RLS, and audit-integrity findings while constraining automation from auto-merging or destructive actions. Together they show how the auth and RLS guarantees you traced through the tour are kept honest as the codebase evolves.
tour.languageLesson: GitHub Actions workflows use 'on' triggers (push/pull_request), 'jobs' that run in parallel, and 'steps' that run sequentially. Caching the dependency install and reusing it across runs is the standard way to keep CI fast.
